name: Powerland
alexConclusions:
Overall, their business model as a CPO is very similar to an installer. For them most likely making money means more installations, more sold hardware, and more maintenance contracts. Roaming, BESS, Day Ahead trading — are all probably ways to sign contracts, but not that valuable or a focus in terms of revenue making. If they have only 400k stations through roaming they probably have some cheap managed eMSP, just so they can provide the service. I doubt they are making any money on the roaming. CPMS they are using is probably either custom or some very cheap local one if I have to guess, as I can't recognise the platform by the mobile app screenshots.

We always have to consider that they don't want to pay high monthly retainer fees or connectors fees, as they probably don't charge much on the monthly subscriptions from their own clients, as the way they conduct their business is through the installer lens.

So if you want to get this CPO — focus on new ways for them to acquire customers, even though they already have a lot of integrations with BESS and day ahead trading. Another one could be operational madness around managing thousands of clients — I doubt they are using a good CPMS that can manage many clients. But again, the price here will be a leading factor as most likely they are not making a lot of money on the monthly subscriptions.

Overall, I wouldn't chase this client much, unless I get more intel that they are actually making money on monthly subscriptions, or roaming, or the added services like BESS and day ahead trading, which I doubt.

funding.label: Mindset
funding.value: "We installed gas stations, now we install EV chargers" — capex-heavy, margin-on-hardware model

salesSignals.text: Installer-first mindset. Revenue = hardware + maintenance contracts. Monthly software fees are seen as a cost, not an investment. Price sensitivity will be high.

Operational complexity at scale. Managing many client sites from a single platform is genuinely difficult. If they're using a cheap/custom CPMS, this could be a real pain point as the client base grows.

New revenue streams as an angle. BESS, Day Ahead trading, roaming — these are already on their radar. A CPMS that unlocks better roaming revenue or dynamic pricing could be positioned as a growth tool, not just an ops tool.
Employee home charging reimbursement is already offered — suggesting some CPMS capability exists. Worth understanding what that looks like today and where it breaks down.

openQuestions: What CPMS are they currently using? Is it vendor-supplied with the hardware, or independently chosen?
How much revenue do they actually generate from monthly subscriptions vs hardware sales?
Is the Day Ahead arbitrage feature generating meaningful ROI, or is it a sales tool only?
How many active client sites are they managing — and what are the biggest ops headaches?
Are they planning to expand roaming and improve driver-facing services, or is that not a priority?

synthesis.verdictText: Powerland operates with an installer mindset — revenue comes from hardware and maintenance, not software subscriptions. Monthly CPMS fees will be scrutinised heavily. Not worth active pursuit unless there is confirmed evidence that subscription/roaming revenue is growing and operational complexity is becoming a bottleneck.

hypotheses.label: Hypothesis 1 — Software is a cost centre, not a growth lever
hypotheses.text: Coming from a gas station installation background, Vandotec/Powerland will view CPMS as a necessary overhead rather than a strategic tool. The conversation needs to reframe CPMS as a revenue enabler (roaming, better utilisation, customer retention) to get traction.

hypotheses.label: Hypothesis 2 — Multi-client management is the latent pain point
hypotheses.text: If they're managing hundreds of client sites from a weak platform, the operational pain grows non-linearly. A CPMS demo focused on multi-site client management (white-label portals, per-client billing) might resonate more than driver app features.

uncertainties: Monthly subscription revenue volume unknown — if it's negligible, there's no business case for a premium CPMS.
CPMS identity unknown — if bundled with hardware (e.g. Smappee's platform), switching has added friction.
Company size and team structure unclear — procurement decisions may sit with a single technical founder.

contacts.name: Vandotec Group contact
contacts.title: CTO / Head of Operations
contacts.note: Identify via LinkedIn — look for technical or product ownership roles within Powerland specifically.

synthesis.contactsNote: Approach via the operational angle — not strategy. The person who manages client sites day-to-day is the most likely champion.
